name: GLIOBLASTOMA MULTIFORME

demographics.Incidence: most common primary brain tumor; 50% of all intracranial tumors; 1-2% of all
demographics.Age: all ages; peak incidence at 65-75 years; M÷F = 3÷2; more frequently in whites
demographics.Genetics: Turcot syndrome, neurofibromatosis type 1, Li-Fraumeni syndrome (familial
demographics.Path: multilobulated appearance; quite extensive vasogenic edema (transudation through
demographics.Histo: highly cellular, often bizarrely pleomorphic / undifferentiated multipolar astrocytes;

dominant_finding: almost always ring blush of variable thickness: multiscalloped ("garland"), round / ovoid;
distribution: frontal + temporal gliomas tend to invade basal ganglia
discriminating_features: almost always ring blush of variable thickness: multiscalloped ("garland"), round / ovoid;
readily crosses midline = "butterfly" glioma (clue: invasion of septum pellucidum)
frontal + temporal gliomas tend to invade basal ganglia
may invade pia, arachnoid and dura (mimicking meningioma)

general: readily crosses midline = "butterfly" glioma (clue: invasion of septum pellucidum)
frontal + temporal gliomas tend to invade basal ganglia
may invade pia, arachnoid and dura (mimicking meningioma)
NECT: inhomogeneous low-density mass with irregular shape + poorly defined margins
considerable mass effect → compression + displacement of ventricles, cisterns, brain
rarely calcifies (if coexistent with lower-grade glioma / after radio- or chemotherapy)
CECT: almost always ring blush of variable thickness: multiscalloped ("garland"), round / ovoid;
sedimentation level ← cellular debris / hemorrhage / accumulated contrast material in
MR: poorly defined lesion with some mass effect / vasogenic edema / heterogeneity
hemorrhage (= hypointensity on T2WI and T2*WI)
T1WI + gadolinium-DTPA enhancement separates tumor nodules from surrounding edema,
ANGIO: wildly irregular neovascularity + early draining veins

clinical.Prognosis: 16-18 months postoperative survival (frequent tumor recurrence ← during surgery
